description: Generating particles (balls) in PFC 5.0 using ball generate and ball distribute commands. Covers syntax, porosity-based generation, size distributions, and practical examples.

sections.heading: ball generate — No-Overlap Generation
sections.content:
Generates balls with no initial overlap. More computationally expensive for dense packings, but starts from a valid mechanical state.

Syntax:
  ball generate radius r1 r2 number N box x1 x2 [y1 y2] [z1 z2] [tries T]

  radius r1 r2   — min and max radius (uniform random distribution between these values)
  number N       — target number of balls to generate
  box x1 x2 ...  — bounding box for generation
  tries T        — max placement attempts per ball (default 50000); higher values help denser packings

The generator randomly attempts to place non-overlapping balls within the box. If tries are exhausted before placing N balls (due to dense packing), fewer balls are generated. The user should check the actual number placed.

Example:
  ball generate radius 1.0 1.4 box -5.0 5.0 number 30

For 2D simulations (PFC 2D), omit the z-range:
  ball generate radius 0.5 0.8 box -10.0 10.0 -10.0 10.0 number 100 tries 100000

sections.heading: ball distribute — Porosity-Based Generation
sections.content:
Generates balls to achieve a target porosity within a box. Balls may initially overlap; overlapping contacts are resolved during cycling.

Syntax:
  ball distribute porosity P radius r1 r2 box x1 x2 y1 y2 z1 z2

  porosity P      — target porosity (0.0-1.0). Porosity = 1 - (ball volume / box volume)
  radius r1 r2    — min and max radius range
  box x1 x2 ...   — bounding box

This is the preferred method for creating dense granular packings. The generator calculates how many balls of the given radius distribution are needed to fill the box volume at the target porosity, then places them randomly (with possible overlap).

Example:
  ball distribute porosity 0.2 radius 1.0e-3 1.5e-3 box -0.025 0.025 -0.025 0.025 -0.04 0.04

sections.heading: ball distribute — Default Porosity Values
sections.content:
PFC 5.0 ball distribute default porosity:
  - 2D: 0.16 (tight, mono-disperse packing of disks)
  - 3D: 0.359 (tight, mono-disperse packing of spheres)

The default applies when no porosity keyword is given.

Note: PFC 5.0 uses 'numbin' (singular, NOT 'numbins' or 'numbins') for specifying multiple size fractions:

  ball distribute porosity 0.36 radius 1.0 3.0 box -10 10 \
    numbin 2 \
    bin 1 radius 1.0 1.6 volumefraction 0.25 group small \
    bin 2 radius 2.0 3.0 volumefraction 0.75 group large

Keyword: 'numbin' vs PFC7 'number-bins', 'volumefraction' vs PFC7 'volume-fraction'

sections.heading: ball distribute — Multiple Size Fractions
sections.content:
Use multiple size bins to create a graded particle size distribution (PSD).

Syntax:
  ball distribute porosity P radius r1 r2 box ... numbin N bin i radius r1i r2i volumefraction fi ...

  numbin N                    — number of size fractions
  bin i radius r1i r2i        — radius range for bin i
  bin i ... volumefraction fi — volume fraction for bin i (must sum to 1.0)
  gauss                       — optional: use Gaussian distribution within each bin (default is uniform)

Example with 4 size fractions:
  ball distribute porosity 0.15 radius 1e-3 1.8e-3 box -0.024 0.024 -0.049 0.049 -0.024 0.024 numbin 4 \
    bin 1 radius 1e-3 1.3e-3 volumefraction 0.25 \
    bin 2 radius 1.3e-3 1.6e-3 volumefraction 0.50 \
    bin 3 radius 1.6e-3 1.8e-3 volumefraction 0.25

Note: In PFC 5.0, the keywords are 'numbin' (not 'number-bins') and 'volumefraction' (not 'volume-fraction'). PFC 7.0+ uses the hyphenated forms.

sections.heading: ball distribute with Gaussian Distribution
sections.content:
Add the 'gauss' keyword to use a Gaussian (normal) distribution instead of uniform within radius bounds.

Syntax:
  ball distribute porosity P radius r1 r2 box ... numbin N gauss bin 1 radius ...

With 'gauss', the mean radius is (r1 + r2)/2 and the standard deviation is (r2 - r1)/4, meaning approximately 95% of balls fall within the specified radius range.

Example:
  ball distribute porosity 0.2 radius 2e-4 6e-4 box ... numbin 3 gauss \
    bin 1 radius 2e-4 3e-4 volumefraction 0.2 \
    bin 2 radius 3e-4 5e-4 volumefraction 0.6 \
    bin 3 radius 5e-4 6e-4 volumefraction 0.2

sections.heading: Post-Generation Commands
sections.content:
After distributing or generating balls, common follow-up commands:

Set ball attributes:
  ball attribute density D    — set density (used for mass calculation)
  ball attribute damp d       — set local damping coefficient (default ~0.7)

Set initial conditions (canonical form):
  ball attribute xvelocity 0  — zero x-velocity (canonical; 'xvel' works as abbreviation)
  ball attribute yvelocity 0  — zero y-velocity
  ball attribute zvelocity 0  — zero z-velocity
  ball attribute spin 0 0 0   — zero spin on all 3 axes (3D: fx fy fz)

NOTE: 'spin' takes 3 components in 3D (fx fy fz), 1 component in 2D (fz).
  Using 'spin 0' in 3D sets only fx=0; fy and fz retain previous values.
  Always specify all components: 'spin 0 0 0' for 3D.

Cycle to equilibrium:
  cycle 1000 calm 50          — cycle 1000 steps, calm every 50 steps
  solve aratio 1e-4           — solve to average ratio (max unbalanced / avg contact force) < 1e-4

Apply gravity:
  set gravity 0 0 -9.81       — apply gravitational acceleration (z-direction)

Note: The 'calm' keyword in the cycle command zeros ball velocities every N steps, which helps dissipate kinetic energy during initial packing.

sections.heading: Practical Notes
sections.content:
- Prior to ball generation, set the random seed with 'set random N' for reproducibility.
- 'ball generate' is recommended for sparse packings (low particle count or large box).
- 'ball distribute' is better for dense packings (e.g., soil/rock samples with porosity 0.1-0.4).
- After 'ball distribute', cycle with 'calm' to dissipate energy from overlapping contacts.
- The domain extent must be set large enough to contain the generation box, or balls may be deleted at the domain boundary. Use 'domain extent ... condition destroy' to delete escapees.
- The 'set random' seed affects both 'ball generate' and 'ball distribute' (and also 'wall generate'). Different seeds produce different packings.
- In PFC 5.0, 'ball generate' and 'ball distribute' are separate commands. In PFC 7.0+, they have been unified under a single command with sub-options.
